text: I'm not a hardware designer, but I've had classes on HW design and I've done quite a bit of research on it. In general, does it make sense to design hardware that supports functional languages? Correct me if I'm wrong here, but it seems to me that the most expensive operation for languages like clojure are around their <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Persistent_data_structure|data structures> (even though they are really fast). Could fixed function hardware be designed to make persistant data structures a winning choice? e.g. could be stuff like improving cache synchronization. I don't know where this would fit best though - would this type of FF HW be best placed within the cpu core, or perhaps alongside the ram? Just spit balling here, hopefully someone here knows enough to say weather this is dumb or a possible idea.

text:
I'm reminded of this recent <https://twitter.com/Catfish_Man/status/1326238434235568128|tweet> from Apple engineer Dave Smith about the perf of the M1 chip:

> fun fact: retaining and releasing an NSObject takes ~30 nanoseconds on current gen Intel, and ~6.5 nanoseconds on an M1
> 
> …and ~14 nanoseconds on an M1 emulating an Intel :innocent: 
They designed the M1 chip to do really well at object retain/release because Swift & Obj-C use reference counting extensively (rather than, say, some sort of full-blown GC).

Would be interesting to know what work they did to the hardware to accelerate that particular pattern. Like you said — is it just in the CPU, or is it affected by memory latency, or..?

Would also be interesting to know whether other languages could be pulled along in the slipstream of that particular HW optimization.

Would be interesting to know what work they did to the hardware to accelerate that particular pattern. Like you said — is it just in the CPU, or is it affected by memory latency, or..?

Would also be interesting to know whether other languages could be pulled along in the slipstream of that particular HW optimization.

text: I think it is a rabbit hole. Hardware is inherently stateful, state machines are a good model to think about it. There were whole teams trying to come up with Lisp machines and the effort is well documented elsewhere but they ultimately failed to get traction (there's also a distinction to be made between "functional" and "immutable"). I feel like stateful hardware with an immutable layer on top is the correct way to model something like Clojure.

text:
Yeah, this has been explored a whole lot. I'd have more citations to provide 10+ years ago when I did my PhD in computer architecture. One thing to note here is that the whole CISC thing that RISC was in response to was a trend of adding special instructions for common patterns in software.

Which is not to say there's nothing to explore here. It's gotten quite unfashionable for 30+ years now. That seems like enough time for another turn of the wheel.

text:
On the one hand: yeah, RISC has definitely had a good run for the past 30 years.

On the other hand: chips are extremely wide now wherever the software is able to make use of that (eg: SIMD, GPU units); SoCs have dedicated image signal processors, ML accelerators, crypto accelerators, video decoding (and sometimes encoding); mobile chips (or, at least, Apple's chips) have heterogeneous cores, some for high-perf and some for low-power.

So there seems to be a move away from simple, undifferentiated compute and a return to specialized hardware aplenty — but this time it's physical specialization, rather than instruction specialization.

Curiously, it seems the opposite might be happening in memory. RAM and VRAM are frequently merged (ie. by M1, modern game consoles, mobile devices), there's the thought that we might see NVRAM combine RAM and storage, I wouldn't be surprised if L3 cache fell out of favour.

On the other hand: chips are extremely wide now wherever the software is able to make use of that (eg: SIMD, GPU units); SoCs have dedicated image signal processors, ML accelerators, crypto accelerators, video decoding (and sometimes encoding); mobile chips (or, at least, Apple's chips) have heterogeneous cores, some for high-perf and some for low-power.

So there seems to be a move away from simple, undifferentiated compute and a return to specialized hardware aplenty — but this time it's physical specialization, rather than instruction specialization.

Curiously, it seems the opposite might be happening in memory. RAM and VRAM are frequently merged (ie. by M1, modern game consoles, mobile devices), there's the thought that we might see NVRAM combine RAM and storage, I wouldn't be surprised if L3 cache fell out of favour.

Not sure how's it is handled when you add the schemas though. Data could be valid in isolation but not when merged together.

text:
Hello Everyone,
My name is Greg and I’m a software engineer at Adzerk, where I get to code in Clojure, doing backend development and data engineering. Big fan of Lisp, Functional Programming and PL topics in general, with a keen interest in how FOC ideas can make data exploration and analysis easier and more accessible for everyone, including non programmers.
Few years ago I had a chance to work on Mathematica inspired custom language with Notebook based UI and backed by Hadoop Data Lake, built as a data exploration platform for a financial institution and it was quite an eye opening experience.
I’m very excited to discover that there is a community of people sharing the dissatisfaction with current state of programming and dedicated to exploring ideas on how to improve its future!

elements.text:

text:
<@U01DUNFS2N4> While most Lisps allow one to tinker with the inner workings of cons cells, it's probably worth noting that the standard list building process of `(cons elem the-list)`  (construct a new list with `elem` as the head and `the-list` as the tail) does not modify `the-list` in any way, allowing other reference holders to continue as if nothing has happened. It's a form of "unenforced immutability," if you will.

You can read about how this works in the context of Common Lisp here:
<http://blog.thezerobit.com/2012/07/21/immutable-persistent-data-structures-in-common-lisp.html>

text:
Just published a new essay y’all might find interesting, on why code is an important medium for communication (no new ideas for this forum, of course :slightly_smiling_face: ) in many cases we get access to more precision than mathematical notation offers. but much more powerfully, code can power many different representations of some idea:

1. the symbols of the themselves
2. mathematical notation generated from the symbols
3. animations
4. graphical renderings 
alternate title might have been “Code as a better serialization format for scientific ideas”. Enjoy!
<https://roadtoreality.substack.com/p/increasing-our-channel-capacity>

attachments.title: Increasing our Channel Capacity
attachments.text: Code as Communication, and the kinesthetics of powerful ideas.

text:
I have done two small efforts in this direction. First one is to make Javascript programmable in my mother tongue using some regex hacks: <https://makers-of-kerala.com/thengascript/>

Another one was a plan to use Lisp macros: <https://twitter.com/prathyvsh/status/635733805567840256?s=21>

If you are interested in looking at such efforts, then Yor lang: <https://github.com/anoniscoding/yorlang> and Wenyan: <https://ide.wy-lang.org/> are two nice projects.

If its for a more fundamental base from which to internationalize the base language, think there was something that leveraged Jetbrains MPS platform.
